threats.title: Token1 Premium Sign Inversion in computeNAV Systematically Distorts All Share Pricing
threats.hypothesis_description: In computeNAV, token1 premium exposure is computed as longPremium.leftSlot() - shortPremium.leftSlot(), which inverts the sign relative to token0's correct computation of shortPremium.rightSlot() - longPremium.rightSlot(). When the vault holds net-short positions earning token1 premium (shortPremium.leftSlot > longPremium.leftSlot), the NAV is understated because earned premium is subtracted instead of added. Conversely, when the vault owes token1 premium, NAV is overstated. This bug affects every deposit and withdrawal fulfillment, silently mispricing all shares.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a hardcoded logic error in the NAV computation path that affects the core economic invariant: correct asset valuation. Every fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals call uses this flawed NAV, meaning all share issuance and redemption prices are wrong whenever token1 premiums are nonzero.

proof_of_signal.pattern: poolExposure0 = shortPremium.rightSlot() - longPremium.rightSlot() (correct); poolExposure1 = longPremium.leftSlot() - shortPremium.leftSlot() (inverted sign)

preconditions: Vault holds positions in at least one Panoptic pool
Net token1 premium is nonzero (either owed or earned)
fulfillDeposits or fulfillWithdrawals is called

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Token0 premium uses short-long but token1 uses long-short with no documented justification for asymmetry
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — bug is unconditional in all NAV computations
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — every fulfillment call uses the wrong NAV

operational_questions: What is the typical magnitude of net token1 premiums for vault positions?
How much NAV distortion does this create in realistic portfolio compositions?
Can a strategic position be constructed to maximize the premium sign discrepancy?
validation_requirements: Compute NAV with corrected sign and compare to current output for test positions
Verify the semantics of shortPremium/longPremium from PanopticPool.getAccumulatedFeesAndPositionsData
Quantify share mispricing across deposit and withdrawal fulfillment scenarios

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Vault accumulates net short token1 premium through normal options selling
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: NAV is understated, causing fulfillDeposits to issue excess shares to depositors (diluting existing holders)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Alternatively, during withdrawal fulfillment with overstated NAV (when vault owes token1 premium), withdrawers receive excess assets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No attacker action needed — the bug operates continuously during normal vault operations

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None — affects economic correctness, not access control
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All share pricing is systematically wrong; depositors and withdrawers receive incorrect amounts
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires accountant code fix and retroactive accounting correction for affected epochs
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: fulfillDeposits, fulfillWithdrawals, and all downstream executeDeposit/executeWithdrawal

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Always active when token1 premiums are nonzero

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Compare NAV output with manual calculation using corrected premium signs
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected share pricing discrepancies after epochs with active premium accumulation

threats.title: Per-Pool NAV Clamping to Zero Overstates Vault Value by Ignoring Underwater Pool Debt
threats.hypothesis_description: computeNAV applies Math.max(poolExposure0 + poolExposure1, 0) per pool before summing. When any pool has negative combined exposure (underwater position), its debt is discarded instead of reducing the total NAV. If Pool A = +100 and Pool B = -80, real NAV should be 20, but the code returns max(100,0) + max(-80,0) = 100. This inflated NAV affects all share pricing during deposit and withdrawal fulfillment.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a structural logic error in NAV aggregation. The per-pool clamping creates a systematic upward bias in NAV whenever any pool position is underwater, directly impacting the fairness of share issuance and redemption prices.

proof_of_signal.pattern: nav += uint256(Math.max(poolExposure0 + poolExposure1, 0)); — per-pool clamping instead of aggregate sum then clamp

preconditions: Vault has positions in two or more pools
At least one pool has negative combined exposure (underwater)
fulfillDeposits or fulfillWithdrawals is called

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Math.max per pool silently discards debt from underwater positions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager structures positions across pools; honest manager with underwater pool still triggers bug
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Active whenever any pool position is underwater during fulfillment

operational_questions: How likely are multi-pool vaults to have one underwater pool in normal market conditions?
What is the maximum NAV overstatement possible with realistic pool allocations?
Does the inflated NAV cause depositors to receive fewer shares or withdrawers to receive excess assets?
validation_requirements: Create test scenario with one profitable and one underwater pool, compare per-pool vs aggregate clamping
Verify that deposit fulfillment with inflated NAV dilutes new depositors
Verify that withdrawal fulfillment with inflated NAV overpays withdrawers

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Vault naturally holds positions across multiple Panoptic pools
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Market moves cause one pool to go underwater while another remains profitable
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: NAV is overstated — depositors receive fewer shares (diluted), withdrawers may receive excess assets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: When the underwater pool's debt is eventually settled, remaining shareholders absorb the full loss
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None required — triggered by market conditions

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Systematic share mispricing; value transferred from depositors/remaining holders to withdrawers
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix and potentially retroactive corrections
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All fulfillment operations when multi-pool positions exist

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None for natural occurrence; basic DeFi knowledge for strategic exploitation
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During market volatility affecting different pools asymmetrically

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Track per-pool exposure values to identify underwater pools
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: NAV reported higher than expected given known position P&L
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Withdrawal spikes when one pool is known to be underwater

threats.title: Epoch Rollover Rounding Causes User-Level Share Over-Minting vs Epoch-Level Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: When fulfillDeposits partially fulfills an epoch, the aggregate unfulfilled remainder is rolled to the next epoch's assetsDeposited. When individual users call executeDeposit, each user's pro-rata fulfilled amount is rounded down via mulDiv, making individual rollovers slightly larger than the aggregate. The sum of user-level rollovers exceeds the epoch-level assetsDeposited for the next epoch. In the next epoch, users collectively claim more shares than were added to totalSupply, creating unbacked shares. With many small depositors and low fulfillment ratios, the discrepancy becomes material (demonstrated: 10 users depositing 1 each with 1/10 fulfillment creates 10x rollover discrepancy).
threats.investigation_rationale: The executeDeposit function updates only user-level queuedDeposit for rollovers but never updates the epoch-level depositEpochState. This structural mismatch means epoch-level denominators systematically undercount actual user claims in subsequent epochs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: executeDeposit computes userAssetsDeposited = mulDiv(queuedAmount, assetsFulfilled, assetsDeposited) then rolls remainder to epoch+1 via queuedDeposit[user][epoch+1] += assetsRemaining without updating depositEpochState[epoch+1].assetsDeposited

preconditions: Partial fulfillment of a deposit epoch (assetsToFulfill < assetsDeposited)
Multiple users have deposits in the partially fulfilled epoch
Individual deposit amounts are small relative to total (maximizes rounding)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: executeDeposit updates per-user rollover but not epoch-level assetsDeposited for next epoch
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager triggers partial fulfillment; issue manifests during permissionless executeDeposit calls
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every partial fulfillment epoch with multiple depositors

operational_questions: What is the maximum share over-minting in realistic scenarios (deposit sizes, user counts)?
Does the over-minting in epoch N+1 offset unclaimed shares from epoch N (conservation)?
Can this be weaponized by creating many small deposits to maximize rounding?
validation_requirements: Simulate partial fulfillment with N users, track sum(queuedDeposit[*][epoch+1]) vs depositEpochState[epoch+1].assetsDeposited
Verify whether total shares minted across all executeDeposit calls exceeds sharesReceived from fulfillment
Test with extreme parameters: many small deposits, low fulfillment ratio

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create multiple accounts with small deposits (1 unit each)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for partial fulfillment with low ratio (e.g., 1 out of N fulfilled)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute deposits for all accounts — each gets 0 from current epoch, full amount rolls to next
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: In next epoch, each account claims proportional shares using understated epoch-level denominator, collectively minting excess shares
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Multiple accounts controlled by attacker; no special privileges needed

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Share supply inflation; existing shareholders diluted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Difficult — requires tracking per-epoch discrepancies across all users
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All executeDeposit calls after partial fulfillments

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — attacker controls multiple deposit accounts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Track sum of user deposits per epoch vs epoch-level assetsDeposited after rollovers
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: totalSupply diverging from expected sum of fulfilled shares
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Many small deposits from related addresses

threats.title: totalAssets Arithmetic Underflow Permanently Bricks Epoch Fulfillment When Vault Is Underwater
threats.hypothesis_description: Both fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals compute totalAssets = accountant.computeNAV(...) + 1 - assetsDeposited - reservedWithdrawalAssets. If the NAV drops below assetsDeposited + reservedWithdrawalAssets - 1 (due to investment losses), this arithmetic underflows and reverts. The manager cannot advance either deposit or withdrawal epochs, trapping all queued deposits and pending withdrawals. The only recovery path is manager calling cancelDeposit/cancelWithdrawal for each user individually.
threats.investigation_rationale: The vault invests deposited assets (via manage()) while keeping them in the accounting system as pending. If investments lose value, the subtraction underflows, creating a hard block on all epoch advancement without any user-accessible recovery mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalAssets = accountant.computeNAV(...) + 1 - epochState.assetsDeposited - reservedWithdrawalAssets — underflows if NAV < deposits + reserved - 1

preconditions: Vault has active investment positions that incur losses
NAV drops below the sum of pending deposit assets and reserved withdrawal assets
Pending deposits or withdrawals exist requiring fulfillment

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No graceful degradation when vault is underwater — hard revert instead of capped fulfillment
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager must individually cancel each user's deposit/withdrawal for recovery
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During market downturns affecting vault positions

operational_questions: How quickly can the manager cancel all pending deposits/withdrawals during a crisis?
Can the vault reach underwater state if manage() is used to invest pending deposit funds?
Is there a scenario where both fulfillment AND cancellation are blocked?
validation_requirements: Simulate loss scenario where NAV < assetsDeposited + reservedWithdrawalAssets
Verify that cancelDeposit and cancelWithdrawal still work when fulfillment is bricked
Test recovery path with large number of pending users

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Vault has pending deposits and existing positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Market moves cause position losses, reducing NAV below threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager attempts to fulfill deposits/withdrawals but transactions revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users' assets are trapped; recovery requires per-user cancellation by manager
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: No attacker needed — natural market risk

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of deposit and withdrawal processing
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Manager must manually cancel each user; if manager is unresponsive, funds are permanently locked

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor NAV relative to assetsDeposited + reservedWithdrawalAssets

threats.title: Uninitialized feeWallet (address(0)) Permanently Blocks All Profitable Withdrawal Executions
threats.hypothesis_description: The constructor does not initialize feeWallet, leaving it at address(0). performanceFeeBps is immutable and set at construction. If performanceFeeBps > 0 and setFeeWallet is never called (or called with address(0)), executeWithdrawal attempts SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer to address(0) for any withdrawal with profit. Most ERC20 tokens revert on transfer to address(0), permanently blocking all profitable withdrawal executions until owner sets a valid feeWallet.
threats.investigation_rationale: There is no validation in the constructor that feeWallet is set when performanceFeeBps > 0, and no check in executeWithdrawal that feeWallet != address(0) before attempting the fee transfer. This creates a deployment footgun that blocks core functionality.
deployment_assumptions: setFeeWallet is called with a valid non-zero address before any profitable withdrawals are executed
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script provided; constructor does not set feeWallet; no validation in executeWithdrawal

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (performanceFee > 0) { SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(underlyingToken, feeWallet, performanceFee); } — feeWallet defaults to address(0), no validation

preconditions: performanceFeeBps > 0 (set immutably in constructor)
feeWallet == address(0) (never set via setFeeWallet)
A user's withdrawal has positive profit (assetsToWithdraw > withdrawnBasis)
Underlying token reverts on transfer to address(0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Constructor sets performanceFeeBps but not feeWallet; no coupling between the two
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner must remember to call setFeeWallet post-deployment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From deployment until setFeeWallet is called

operational_questions: Does the deployment process include a setFeeWallet call?
Does the underlying token revert on transfer to address(0)?
How many users could be affected before the issue is discovered?
validation_requirements: Verify feeWallet state after deployment
Test executeWithdrawal with feeWallet=address(0) and positive performanceFee
Check if SafeTransferLib handles address(0) gracefully

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Vault deployed with performanceFeeBps > 0 but feeWallet not set
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users deposit, vault generates returns, epoch fulfilled
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User calls executeWithdrawal with profit — reverts on fee transfer to address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All profitable withdrawals are blocked until owner intervenes

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low — owner calls setFeeWallet; but users may lose trust

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Immediately after first profitable withdrawal attempt

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Check feeWallet state variable after deployment

threats.title: Zero Fulfillment Amount Creates Permanently Unexecutable Epoch with Trapped User Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: fulfillDeposits(0, ...) and fulfillWithdrawals(0, ..., ...) are not guarded against zero input. A zero fulfillment advances the epoch, recording assetsFulfilled=0 and sharesReceived=0 (or sharesFulfilled=0 and assetsReceived=0). When users call executeDeposit or executeWithdrawal for that epoch, mulDiv computes X * 0 / 0 which reverts (division by zero). Users' deposits/withdrawals in that epoch become permanently unexecutable. The rollover mechanism in executeDeposit/executeWithdrawal is also unreachable due to the revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing input validation on the fulfillment amount parameter. Even an honest manager could accidentally pass 0, creating an unrecoverable epoch. The epoch has already advanced, so the broken state cannot be corrected.

proof_of_signal.pattern: fulfillDeposits has no require(assetsToFulfill > 0); executeDeposit uses mulDiv(queuedAmount, assetsFulfilled=0, assetsDeposited) which is 0, then mulDiv(0, sharesReceived=0, assetsFulfilled=0) → div-by-zero revert

preconditions: Manager calls fulfillDeposits(0, ...) or fulfillWithdrawals(0, ..., ...)
Users have queued deposits or withdrawals in that epoch

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No zero-check on fulfillment amount parameter
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager must call with zero amount (accidental or intentional)

operational_questions: Is there any off-chain validation preventing zero fulfillment?
Can the broken epoch be bypassed by any mechanism?
Are there any recovery paths for users stuck in a zero-fulfilled epoch?
validation_requirements: Call fulfillDeposits(0, ...) and verify epoch state
Attempt executeDeposit for the zero-fulfilled epoch and confirm revert
Verify that cancelDeposit cannot help since epoch has already advanced

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Manager (accidentally or maliciously) calls fulfillDeposits(0, managerInput)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Epoch advances, recording 0 for assetsFulfilled and sharesReceived
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users attempt executeDeposit — reverts on division by zero
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users' deposits are trapped; only rollover aggregate was forwarded but per-user claims are unreachable

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All deposits/withdrawals in affected epoch permanently frozen
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery — epoch cannot be re-fulfilled; funds may be permanently lost
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: executeDeposit, executeWithdrawal for the affected epoch

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor fulfillment calls for zero amount parameters

threats.title: Manager Systematically Biases NAV Within maxPriceDeviation Band for Value Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: The manager supplies managerPrices for pool valuations, which are validated only against TWAP within maxPriceDeviation. The manager can consistently choose tick prices at the extreme of the deviation band, inflating NAV during deposit fulfillment (diluting depositors) or deflating NAV during withdrawal fulfillment (underpaying withdrawers). The manager-supplied prices are used to compute liquidity amounts via getAmountsForLiquidity, directly controlling NAV output within the allowed band.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deviation band creates a quantifiable extraction opportunity. If maxPriceDeviation is 100 ticks (~1%), the manager can consistently shift NAV by ~1% on each fulfillment, extracting value from depositors or withdrawers over repeated epochs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Manager-provided poolPrice validated against TWAP with maxPriceDeviation tolerance; same price used in getAmountsForLiquidity for position valuation

preconditions: Manager provides prices at the extreme of maxPriceDeviation band
Vault has significant liquidity positions where tick price affects valuation
maxPriceDeviation is set to a non-trivial value

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Manager-provided prices used for valuation create controllable NAV bias within allowed range
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager controls price input; no mechanism forces use of TWAP midpoint

operational_questions: What is the typical maxPriceDeviation configured for production pools?
How much NAV shift does one full deviation band create for typical position compositions?
Does the deviation apply multiplicatively across pool price and token conversion prices?
validation_requirements: Compute NAV at TWAP midpoint vs at deviation band extremes for representative positions
Quantify cumulative extraction over multiple epochs
Check if conversion price deviation compounds with pool price deviation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: During deposit fulfillment, manager provides prices that maximize NAV (fewer shares per asset for depositors)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: During withdrawal fulfillment, manager provides prices that minimize NAV (fewer assets per share for withdrawers)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager or colluding party captures the difference through their own deposit/withdrawal timing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeated over many epochs, systematic extraction accumulates
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Manager cooperation — fully trusted role per documentation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Systematic underpricing of user shares on both deposit and withdrawal sides
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Difficult to detect on-chain; historical recalculation needed

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — manager makes the choice unilaterally
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Intermediate — understand tick math and NAV impact

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Compare manager-provided prices to TWAP midpoint across historical fulfillments
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Consistent directional bias in manager price selection
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Manager always choosing high/low end of deviation band

threats.title: Double Rounding in executeDeposit Systematically Shorts Users on Shares
threats.hypothesis_description: executeDeposit performs two consecutive mulDiv operations: first computing userAssetsDeposited = mulDiv(queuedAmount, assetsFulfilled, assetsDeposited), then sharesReceived = mulDiv(userAssetsDeposited, sharesReceived, assetsFulfilled). Each mulDiv rounds down independently, compounding rounding error. A single-step mulDiv(queuedAmount, sharesReceived, assetsDeposited) would be more precise. The difference is up to 1 share per user per epoch, and these dust shares are permanently locked (added to totalSupply via fulfillment but never assigned to any user).
threats.investigation_rationale: Systematic downward bias in share allocation to users. While individually small, across many users and epochs this represents a permanent loss of value to depositors and an equally permanent inflation of share price for remaining holders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Two consecutive mulDiv: userAssetsDeposited = mulDiv(queued, fulfilled, deposited); sharesReceived = mulDiv(userAssets, shares, fulfilled) — double floor rounding

preconditions: Any user calls executeDeposit
Rounding occurs in either mulDiv step

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Two-step mulDiv where single-step would suffice and be more accurate
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — affects all users on every executeDeposit

operational_questions: What is the maximum per-user rounding loss in realistic deposit sizes?
Over how many epochs does the cumulative loss become material?
Can the manager exploit this by calling repeated partial fulfillments?
validation_requirements: Compare two-step vs single-step mulDiv results across deposit amount ranges
Quantify cumulative orphaned shares over simulated epoch sequences

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Natural operation — users call executeDeposit after epoch fulfillment
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Each user loses up to 1 share per epoch due to double rounding
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Orphaned shares accumulate in totalSupply but belong to no user
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Manager could potentially trigger repeated partial fulfillments to maximize rounding occurrences
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for natural occurrence; manager cooperation for amplification

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Gradual totalSupply inflation without corresponding user claims

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Track totalSupply vs sum(balanceOf) + pending amounts over time
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Growing gap between totalSupply and actual outstanding shares

threats.title: Manager Drains Reserved Withdrawal Assets via manage() Arbitrary Call
threats.hypothesis_description: The manage() function allows the manager to execute arbitrary calls from the vault, including calling underlyingToken.transfer() to drain all assets including those reserved for fulfilled withdrawal requests (reservedWithdrawalAssets). No check ensures the vault retains sufficient underlying token balance to honor fulfilled withdrawals. Users who have burned shares for withdrawal requests would permanently lose their claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the manager is a trusted role, the manage() function provides capability to break the core solvency invariant (reserved assets backing) with a single call. No guard-rails prevent draining reserved assets even accidentally.

proof_of_signal.pattern: manage() calls target.functionCallWithValue(data, value) with no restriction on target being underlyingToken or amount exceeding free balance

preconditions: Manager is compromised or acts maliciously
reservedWithdrawalAssets > 0 (fulfilled withdrawals pending execution)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: manage() has zero restrictions on targets or amounts; can transfer underlying token including reserved amounts
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager can unilaterally drain all assets
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime manage() is callable with reservedWithdrawalAssets > 0

operational_questions: Is the manager a multisig or EOA?
Are there off-chain monitoring systems for vault balance vs reserved assets?
Could manage() be restricted to disallow direct underlying token transfers?
validation_requirements: Call manage() with underlying token transfer exceeding free balance
Verify executeWithdrawal reverts when vault balance < assetsToWithdraw

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised manager calls manage(underlyingToken, transfer(attacker, fullBalance), 0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All underlying tokens drained including reservedWithdrawalAssets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users call executeWithdrawal — reverts on insufficient balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users have already burned shares; no recovery path

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires off-chain asset recovery; users' shares already burned

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — construct manage() call with token transfer

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large outbound token transfers from vault via manage()

threats.title: Fund Locking Risk: Users Cannot Self-Cancel Deposits or Withdrawals If Manager Is Unavailable
threats.hypothesis_description: cancelDeposit and cancelWithdrawal are restricted to onlyManager. If the manager becomes unresponsive, compromised (with keys lost), or malicious, users have no mechanism to recover their queued assets. Combined with the manager's exclusive control over fulfillment (fulfillDeposits/fulfillWithdrawals), users' deposited underlying tokens and burned shares can be permanently locked with no recovery path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The design creates a single point of failure for all fund recovery. While the owner can replace the manager, there is no timeout mechanism or user-initiated emergency withdrawal path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cancelDeposit and cancelWithdrawal both use onlyManager modifier; no user-callable alternative exists; no timeout-based self-cancellation

preconditions: Manager becomes unresponsive or compromised
Users have queued deposits or withdrawals in current epoch
Owner either cannot or does not replace manager promptly

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No user-accessible emergency exit or timeout mechanism
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: 100% dependency on manager for any fund recovery

operational_questions: Is the manager a multisig with redundancy?
Is there an incident response plan for manager unavailability?
Should a time-delayed self-cancellation mechanism be added?
validation_requirements: Verify no alternative recovery path exists on-chain
Assess owner's ability to rapidly replace manager
Evaluate feasibility of adding timeout-based user cancellation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Manager goes offline (key loss, infrastructure failure, or intentional)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users cannot cancel deposits or withdrawals
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Deposit epoch never fulfilled — funds stuck indefinitely
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Even owner intervention (setManager) requires identifying and trusting a new manager

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All queued deposits and withdrawals frozen
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner must replace manager; no user self-service

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users unable to interact with vault

threats.title: Irreversible Vault Lock Prevents Pool Configuration Updates on Accountant
threats.hypothesis_description: PanopticVaultAccountant.lockVault() is a one-way operation with no unlockVault() function. Once locked, the poolsHash for that vault can never be updated. If the locked configuration references pools that become deprecated, compromised, or require updating, the vault's NAV computation is permanently frozen to the stale configuration. The only recourse is deploying a new accountant (requires vault owner cooperation via setAccountant).
threats.investigation_rationale: Irreversible state transitions without recovery mechanisms create permanent operational risk. Pool configurations may need updating due to protocol upgrades, security incidents, or new pool deployments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lockVault sets vaultLocked[vault] = true; no unlockVault function exists; updatePoolsHash checks if (vaultLocked[vault]) revert VaultLocked()

preconditions: Accountant owner calls lockVault(vaultAddress)
Pool configuration needs updating after lock (deprecation, security issue, new pool)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: One-way lock with no unlock — permanent state transition
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Vault owner can work around by deploying new accountant and calling setAccountant

operational_questions: Is the workaround (deploy new accountant) operationally feasible and timely?
What happens to in-flight fulfillments if accountant is swapped?
Should an unlock mechanism or accountant migration path be added?
validation_requirements: Verify no unlock function exists
Test accountant replacement via setAccountant during active operations
Assess impact of stale pool configuration on NAV accuracy

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Vault is locked with current pool configuration
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Pool referenced in configuration becomes compromised or deprecated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: computeNAV calls to deprecated pool may revert or return stale data
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Vault cannot process fulfillments until accountant is replaced
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: External event (pool deprecation) + accountant owner action

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: NAV computation may fail or be inaccurate with stale pool config
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires new accountant deployment and vault owner cooperation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All fulfillment operations depending on computeNAV

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None for trigger; accountant deployment expertise for recovery

threats.title: Reentrancy in executeWithdrawal Enables Double-Claim via Token Callback and Pre-Fulfilled Next Epoch
threats.hypothesis_description: executeWithdrawal updates state (rolls unfulfilled shares to epoch+1, zeroes current epoch queue) BEFORE making external token transfer calls (SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer to feeWallet and user). If the underlying token has transfer callbacks (ERC777, hooks) and epoch+1 is already fulfilled, a reentrant call to executeWithdrawal(user, epoch+1) would find the user's inflated queuedWithdrawal for epoch+1 (original request + rollover from current epoch) and execute it, claiming the rollover plus original epoch+1 request in one atomic transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: Classic state-before-external-call pattern. The rollover to epoch+1 at line ~300 precedes the token transfers at lines ~307-312. If the transfer triggers a callback, the attacker can re-enter and execute the next epoch's withdrawal with the just-rolled-over shares.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Rollover to queuedWithdrawal[user][epoch+1] at line ~300; external SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer calls at lines ~307-312; no reentrancy guard

preconditions: Underlying token has transfer callback mechanism (ERC777 or similar)
The withdrawal epoch being executed was partially fulfilled (has rollover)
The next epoch (epoch+1) is already fulfilled before the reentrant call
No reentrancy guard is present

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State update (rollover) before external call (token transfer) without reentrancy protection
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any user can trigger via executeWithdrawal
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When partial withdrawal fulfillment + callback token + next epoch fulfilled

operational_questions: Does the target deployment use tokens with transfer callbacks?
Can epoch+1 be fulfilled before all epoch users execute withdrawals?
Is the extra claim bounded by reservedWithdrawalAssets for epoch+1?
validation_requirements: Test with ERC777 token: execute withdrawal with partial fulfillment, verify reentrancy path
Verify whether reservedWithdrawalAssets limits the damage
Check if the reentrant execution changes other users' pro-rata claims

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy vault with ERC777-compatible underlying token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Request withdrawal, wait for partial fulfillment and epoch advance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for next epoch to also be fulfilled
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call executeWithdrawal for first epoch; on token transfer callback, re-enter executeWithdrawal for second epoch with inflated rollover amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Specific token type required; timing of two consecutive epoch fulfillments

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Attacker claims excess withdrawal assets from epoch+1 at other users' expense
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: reservedWithdrawalAssets for epoch+1 is depleted; other users' claims may fail

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium — requires specific token type and epoch timing
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Between two consecutive partial epoch fulfillments

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: executeWithdrawal called multiple times for same user in one transaction
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users deploying contracts as withdrawal recipients
